verbatim_text: We'll represent this theorem as the initial state of the algorithm, as this orange block. Around this state are a bunch of other states corresponding to incorrect proofs for that particular theorem. And somewhere in there is that state corresponding to the correct proof for that theorem. Now, an algorithm's job is to traverse this state space and identify the correct proof. And if it ends up terminating at an incorrect proof, it gets a reward of zero. If it terminates at the correct proof, it gets a reward of one. So now consider training or a family of theorems. Consider a family of PCS problems. Here the problem is about planar graph. It asks, is every planar graph k-colorable? And you train on a family of this problem. So you train on a bunch of these theorems during your standard RL training. Consider the setting where you look at all of the theorems corresponding to the case where k is greater than four. Now, as it turns out, roughly many of them can be solved with a similar enough proof technique that I'll abstractly denote as p6.
